index.tagline: The backbencher who refused to nod like a plastic dog

karran.tagline: The backbencher who refused to nod like a plastic dog
karran.bio: Peter Karran served as MHK for Onchan from the mid-1980s until 2016 — one of the most prolific speakers in modern Manx parliamentary history. He built a reputation as a fierce scrutineer of executive power: challenging ministers, demanding straight answers, and speaking plainly on behalf of ordinary taxpayers long before accountability became fashionable. His warnings about ministerial government and financial regulation often proved prescient — and he was never shy about saying so.

best_bits.quote: You will either be a minister and have power or you might as well be one of these plastic dogs in the back of a car and just nod up and down because you have got no choice, hon. members, but either to nod 'yes' or go.

I detect more than a little power-grabbing, in my opinion, and I warn members that we could live to regret today if we pass this Bill. I suggest that we, hon. members, should defeat this today and review the new form of Government in at least six months' time.

What a disgrace it is that we, the Isle of Man, should be running this sort of thing. You expect them of some of the banana republics — but you do not expect it from a Government which we so proudly sing about and our great heritage in this Heritage Year.

I am equally sure that we will be amending this Bill sooner rather than later and I will not be enjoying having to say that I told you so, because once again this Island will have the smear of financial problems in the future, and that is the last thing that you need when your main seer is the financial sector.

What I am asking is leave to introduce the removal of a small clause in an Act that came out of an age before ministerial government which is fundamental to having the Legislature that is free from executive Government control.
best_bits.context: Treasury Act amendment — leave to introduce

best_bits.quote: We have a responsibility to make sure that we have an efficient health service and we do not have to waste public money on injuries that are self-inflicted, and when you go through a windscreen without wearing a seat belt that is a self-inflicted injury.
best_bits.context: Seat belts and public health debate

best_bits.quote: I have heard nothing in this House today to convince me that we should be speeding up this form of Government and I distrust the almost indecent haste in which this legislation has been introduced.
Mr. Speaker, I would like to know why we are sitting in private. If it is to do with policy then I think it should be in public. I would like to know why we sit in private.
best_bits.context: Early sitting as a new MHK

more_quotes.quote: Vainstyr Loayreyder, would the Chief Minister not agree that the actions ove¡ this whole affair are tantamount to the actions of a banana republic, not a parliamentary democracy, in the scandalous way that you have allowed landowner's properfy to be blown up and a third of an acre has disappea¡ed into the bottom of a government quarry which we do not own the land for, and would the Chief Minisær not also agree that he needs to stop covering up over the sort of scandalous situations where there is a waste of taxpayers' money?

Vainstyr Loayreyder, I picked up the new hospital when no-one would pick it up. (Mr Cretney: Hear, hear.) I warned of many of the problems that were involved with the new hospital and I have found now that the chickens have come home to roost concerning the points where I have been ridiculed, but I do not believe that there was ever any intention of such a proposal by the hon. member. I would find that absolutely outrageous, if that were the case. I would personally say I believe that the National Health Service should be a first-class service for the patients that need that service and I would find it very difficult to be able to justify, as a Labour man, if I am going to put 70 per cent of the new hospital for the private sector. NEW HOSPITAL AT BALLAMONA — RESIDENTIAL ACCOMMODATION -…

Vainstyr Loayreyder, would the Chief Minister not agree that the reason why this was bought and this scandalous waste of taxpayers' money by knocking down this building was done was to silence a senior member of the Manx Ba¡ from objecting to any further development, and that is the reason why, and would the Chief Minister not also agree that if we can throw money at one section of the community for these people orif they have got hyphenated names we can jump through hoops backwa¡ds - can we see that the same sort of priority can be given to landowners that own land for generations being treated the same way, or is it a sin to be Manx?

Vainstyr Loayreyder, a supplementary. Would the Shirveishagh Tashtee not agree with me that the Government should be making representations? It is an absolute scandal that the taxpayer is paying for the likes of Manx Radio –

Vainstyr Loayreyder, would the acting Shirveishagh not agree that it seems an appalling situation that the building has been empty for something like four years? Would the Shirveishagh not agree that the number one priority of his Department in the very near future, and this Hon. House, is going to be employment opportunities? How come a building of this sort of value can be allowed to lie empty for nearly four years? Is that not a disgrace to the taxpayer?

Vainstyr Loayreyder, would the minister agree with me that, as far as the Animal Health Act is concerned and the welfare of battery hens, this House would find it absolutely disgraceful if the taxpayer's money here is being paid out in subsidy for places where they are being cruel to animals? And would she not also agree with me that the time has come for a new Animal Welfare Bill in order to ban battery production on this Island? And would she not also agree that the situation is that her department needs to live in the real world? This Island can never compete on a battery hen system where we are talking of 3,000 hens and they are talking of half-a-million-plus hens, and why can she not wise up and see the situation that this whole practice should be made illegal in this Island?

Vainstyr Loayreyder, would the minister not agree, where there is a significant public money involved, that the public should know about the money? It is taxpayer’s money. Does he not also agree that it is almost impossible to hide from the public any information placed on public record and that persons involved in such transactions are required to be registered by law? Would he expect then that invariably then that some points of information about such transactions, which are placed in the public domain, somehow become commercially sensitive when the issue is the fact that it is in a public register, and would he not agree that what his statements were to the media, from my written response, were incorrect?

Vainstyr Loayreyder, would the Shirveishagh not agree that the audit that is being done, now, is not a technical audit? Would the Shirveishagh not agree that it is not unreasonable that the Council of Ministers and the Ard-Shirveishagh should be considering a technical audit? At the end of the day, this is the last of a long list of scandals of badly paid out money for the taxpayer. Would he go back to the Council of Ministers and report the issue that we should have a technical audit, to get proper value for money, with all the amounts of badly spent money, as far as the MEA is concerned – especially in the light that he will be coming to ask for money from the national reserve, to bail out the MEA to a considerable amount of money, if not between £100 million to £200 million to do so,…
